coredata.dc:title: Granite waste mediated synthesis of polyaniline nanofibers for the catalytic reduction of hazardous organic water toxins
coredata.dc:creator: Lal Meena, Parmeshwar
Kumar Saini, Jitendra
Kumar Surela, Ajay
coredata.dc:description: Annually, a massive quantity of waste in the form of scrap and dust is generated from granite production mines, processing units, and application sites worldwide. In comparison to the generation of waste, the reutilization is quite fewer. Thus, the produced granite wastes which are not disposed of properly litter and cause severe environmental problems related to air, soil, and underground water contamination as well as health-related issues to humans. Hence, it is a necessity to dispose of or reutilize the produced wastes for the generation of useful materials. In the current study granite waste (GW) generated from the marble industries is utilized to synthesize the polyaniline (PANI) nanofibers and used for the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and Rhodamine B (RhB) dye pollutants in presence of NaBH4. The 4-NP and RhB dye were reduced almost completely into harmless products in 40 and 5 min of reaction time, respectively over 15 mg of PANI nanofiber catalyst with NaBH4. The kinetics of the reduction reactions followed pseudo-first-order kinetics and the kinetic rate constant values were estimated to be 0.0488 and 0.958 min−1 for 4-NP and RhB, respectively. As a whole, the current plan of study for the fabrication of PANI using GW can facilitate the disposal of the GW directly, and furthermore, indirectly the produced PANI nanofibers might display striking behavior in exclusion of hazardous water pollutants from wastewater.

head.ce:title: Granite waste mediated synthesis of polyaniline nanofibers for the catalytic reduction of hazardous organic water toxins

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur 302004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Rajasthan, Jaipur 302004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Annually, a massive quantity of waste in the form of scrap and dust is generated from granite production mines, processing units, and application sites worldwide. In comparison to the generation of waste, the reutilization is quite fewer. Thus, the produced granite wastes which are not disposed of properly litter and cause severe environmental problems related to air, soil, and underground water contamination as well as health-related issues to humans. Hence, it is a necessity to dispose of or reutilize the produced wastes for the generation of useful materials. In the current study granite waste (GW) generated from the marble industries is utilized to synthesize the polyaniline (PANI) nanofibers and used for the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and Rhodamine B (RhB) dye pollutants in presence of NaBH4. The 4-NP and RhB dye were reduced almost completely into harmless products in 40 and 5 min of reaction time, respectively over 15 mg of PANI nanofiber catalyst with NaBH4. The kinetics of the reduction reactions followed pseudo-first-order kinetics and the kinetic rate constant values were estimated to be 0.0488 and 0.958 min−1 for 4-NP and RhB, respectively. As a whole, the current plan of study for the fabrication of PANI using GW can facilitate the disposal of the GW directly, and furthermore, indirectly the produced PANI nanofibers might display striking behavior in exclusion of hazardous water pollutants from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Granite is a typical ornamental architectural stone that occurs as the invasive felsic igneous rock which dispensed all over the earth's crust [1–2]. The granites are mainly acidic volcanic rocks that are produced from the compression of magma in profound subversive layers, manifest in granular forms, and produced from inflexible, crystal-structured minerals. The principal minerals that constitute the granite are quartz, along with small amounts of other minerals like mica, pyroxene, hornblende, anorthite, microcline, and secondary minerals such as zircon, tourmaline, garnet, apatite, and magnetite. 10–40% of the granite is mainly composed of quartz, alkaline feldspar present at around 30–60%, mica is 35%, and 10–35% is constituted from dark-colored minerals [3]. Usually, the granites appear in light shades, though they may turn into other shades like pink, gray, or orange which essentially relies on the amount of feldspar and other minerals present in the granites [4].

A large amount of the waste results during granite processing i.e. sawing, polishing, finishing, cutting, and transforming into the specific design of granite rock is estimated to be 20–25 % of global granite [5–7]. The granite-based waste materials can be grouped into two core types one is solid type wastes that are generated during the mining and cutting process and another is sludge type which is mainly produced during the processing of granite [8]. The produced millions of tons of granite dust waste grounds serious environmental problems by polluting soil, air, and groundwater resources [7,9]. The inhalation of fine granite dust particles by humans can cause brutal illness related to the respiratory system, and mixing of granite dust with water create colloid that tremendously contaminates the soil and groundwater [2,5]. In addition, mixing of the granite slurry with soils enduringly changes its pH, conductivity, texture, redox potential, and plummeting moisture, permeability, and organic stuff composition, consequently leading to the worsening of soil quality and productivity [10,11]. Hence, discarding granite waste is a chief worry for human health and environmental shield.

The handling of the huge quantity of waste generated from granite which largely accumulated in stockpiles and yet not being utilized or disposed of is a challenging task for producers as well as researchers [12]. Presently, no proper disposal mechanism for granite wastes is followed, and often accumulated or are landfilled. In comparison to production, the reutilization of waste materials generated from the granite-based industries is reasonably very less. The granite-based scraps created are partly consumed in civil manufacturing works as aggregates whereas the utilization of powdered materials produced from granite is inadequate in subsisting manufacturing processes. The use of granite wastes as aggregates, stuffing materials, or additives in construction materials does not bring any negative influence on the product performance, though the monetary importance reduces consequently the consumption limits [11]. Recently, research reports detected the applications of granite waste in the manufacturing of bricks, tiles, self-compacting concrete, and cement concrete in the form of aggregate and stuffing matter [13,14,15,16,17,18]. As well granite wastes can be utilized in the synthesis of geopolymers [19]. Moreover, composite materials prepared by filling with granite dust used as filler in composites materials capable to enhance the mechanical and permanence aspects of the composites, and the composites utilizing granite dust have been successfully fabricated using acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS) [20,21], polyester composites [22], and polypyrrole composites [23]. Additionally, the waste produced from granite is utilized for the removal of phosphate toxins [24], catalytic degradation of organic dyes in presence of H2O2 [25], and transformation of heavy metal ions into insoluble hydroxides [26]. Thus, the waste materials created from the granites have a wide range of applications in different areas directly or indirectly. In addition, the granite wastes might be used in the transformation of monomers into polymers which can own an exceedingly praiseworthy role in a wide range of fields including environmental remediation especially water treatment.

Water pollutants such as nitroaromatics, organic dyes, metal ions, and bacteria significantly indulge in the water contamination process through numerous mechanisms. These contaminants have to be either eliminated from water resources or transformed into less or nonhazardous products employing effective, economic, and sustainable approaches [27]. Water treatment processes such as adsorption [28,29,30–31], photocatalysis [32,33–36], and catalytic reduction [37,38,39,40,41] are widely employed. Among these chemical reduction methods is one of the suitable processes that is capable to transform extremely perilous heavy metals, synthetic organic dyes, and nitro compounds into less or nonhazardous products [37,38]. In the catalytic reduction of water pollutants especially nitroaromatics and organic dyes, various solid phases pure and composite catalysts such as noble metal-based catalysts [37], metal oxides [35], and polymer-based catalysts [41] are effectively employed, but pure conducting polymer polyaniline (PANI) is not used yet.

PANI is a polymer of aniline monomer that is widely utilized as an adsorbent material for the exclusion of a variety of organic and inorganic water toxins from polluted water [42]. Usually, the PANI is prepared by the oxidative polymerization of aniline monomer employing a variety of oxidants such as ammonium persulphate [43], potassium persulphate [44], sodium persulphate [45], ferric chloride [46], potassium periodate [47]. Therefore, the synthesis of PANI has a wide scope of progression, especially in the selection of an oxidizing agent. Hence, the generation of PANI using natural waste material (granite waste) as a potent oxidizing agent especially black granite since it contains iron would be an environmentally benign and sustainable approach that not only directly helps in the reduction of millions of tons of waste created from the granite-based industries and favor in the get rid of the problems related to granite wastes but also indirectly help in environmental remediation. Additionally, a substance like PANI produced by using GW as an oxidant agent can be effectively used as a heterogeneous catalyst for the reduction of hazardous water pollutants into harmless or less hazardous products.

Therefore, in the present work natural waste generated from granite is utilized for the polymerization of aniline to produce conductive polymer polyaniline which has not been ever utilized for this purpose and the obtained PANI nanofiber is employed for the catalytic reduction of nitrophenol and organic dye in an aqueous medium which is a novel approach. The use of natural resources or the waste material generated from naturally occurring materials is utilized as an oxidant for the preparation of immensely admirable material PANI will contribute toward the sustainability of the environment and minimization of wastes. Thus, the direct use of granite wastes helps in the reduction of pollution load produced in air, soil, and water from GW, and further, the application of synthesized PANI nanofibers for the catalytic reduction of perilous organic water toxins contributes toward the sustainability of the environment.

Aniline (C6H5NH2), Rhodamine B (RhB) dye, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), hydrochloric acid (HCl), nitric acid (HNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethyl alcohol, granite waste, and distilled water were used in the current work. The chemicals utilized in the current work were of analytical grade type. RhB and 4-NP were elected as model toxins to evaluate the catalytic reduction capability of the prepared PANI nanofibers.

To prepare the granite waste extract (GWE), scraps of black granite were collected from the local marble cutting units, broken down into small parts, and washed using tap water followed by distilled water carefully. After that, granite waste pieces were dried for 2 h at 80 °C in an oven. The dried small pieces were grounded into a fine powder using a steel pestle and mortar. Then, the obtained granite waste powder was mixed with conc. HCl and boiled for two hours, cooled, and filtered using Whatman filter paper. The filtrate was again boiled with 10 mL of conc. HNO3 acid for 30 min and the excess of acid was neutralized with 5 N NaOH solution. Thus, the as-obtained granite waste extract was stored at 4 °C for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Granite waste (GW) mediated synthesis of PANI nanofibers
ce:section.ce:para: Polyaniline nanofibers were prepared using aniline in an acidic medium using granite waste extract (GWE) as an oxidant. Briefly, 1 mL of the aniline monomer was dissolved in 100 mL of 1 M HCl solution and magnetically agitated for 60 min at room temperature. Afterward, 100 mL of GWE was mixed with aniline monomer solution drop by drop with consistent stirring to begin the polymerization of aniline. The reaction mixture was constantly stirred for the next two hours at room temperature and then kept undisturbed overnight to complete the polymerization process. A green-colored precipitate was obtained, filtered, and repeatedly washed using distilled water and ethanol till obtaining transparent filtrate. The obtained precipitate was dried in an oven operating at 85 °C for five hours. Fig. 1 illustrates the sketch diagram for the synthesis protocol of granite waste-mediated preparation of PANI nanofibers.

The FESEM micrographs for the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers were obtained from a scanning electron microscopy outfitted with the energy dispersive spectroscopy (FESEM-EDS) (Nova Nano FESEM-450 (FEI)) and utilized for the examination of the morphological features. The EDS spectrum was considered for the elemental composition analysis. The TEM and HRTEM images were consulted for the microstructural and deep morphological analysis of the prepared nanofibers. The TEM and HRTEM were recorded using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (FEI CM12) instrument. The crystallinity and phase purity of the synthesized sample and granite waste powder was evaluated using an X-ray diffraction pattern conducted using a PANalytical XPERT PRO diffractometer with Cu K radiation (λ= 1.5406 Å), and 40 kV and 40 mA between 10 and 70 two theta (2θ). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Perkin Elmer FT-IR) of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers and GW powder was recorded from 400 to 4000 cm−1 wavenumber in the transmittance mode and analyzed to ensure the presence of various functional groups and forms of bonding. Thermal gravimetric analysis for the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers was employed to explore the thermal permanence using a Shimadzu DTA-60H with a heating rate of 10⁰C/min between 0 and 1000⁰C in a nitrogen environment. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analyzer (NOVA touch 2LX) was used to calculate the specific surface area, pore size, and pore distribution for the PANI nanofibers. The absorbance of the prepared PANI nanofibers, and water pollutant solutions before and after the catalytic reduction was measured using a UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600). The zeta potential value for the prepared PANI fibers was determined using a zetasizer (Nano ZSP (ZEN 5600)) Malvern system.

To appraise the catalytic reduction potential of the prepared PANI nanofibers, the catalytic reductions for the 4-NP and RhB dye were carried out. The reduction experiments were conducted in the aqueous medium at normal pH of the solution in presence of NaBH4 solution. 10 mL of (2 mM) 4-NP solution with 10 mL of (0.2 M) NaBH4 solution was mixed and then 10 mg of the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers was added as a catalyst to the mixture and stirred well. After every five min, the absorbance of the 4-NP solution was computed using a spectrophotometer. For the reduction of RhB dye, 10 mL of the 20 mg/L dye solution was taken in the conical flask with 10 mL of 0.2 M NaBH4 solution and an optimized amount of PANI nanofibers was added as a catalyst to the mixture, and the absorbance of the solution was measured after every one min. The catalytic reduction performance of the reduction reactions was determined using the given equation.

where C0 and Ct represent the concentrations of pollutants at initial and time t, respectively, and A0 and At are the absorbance at zero and t time, respectively and k is the rate constant.

FTIR spectrum of granite waste powder is represented in Fig. 2 . The vibration bands observed at 524–1000 cm−1 are attributed to metal oxide bonds. The appearance of two well-defined peaks at 524 and 604 cm−1 is due to the presence of the Fe-O bond in the iron oxide [48,49] and the Si-O bond [50] stretching vibrations, respectively. Peaks near 772 cm−1 can be certified to the Al-O symmetric vibration [49]. The band obtained at 1000 cm−1 is indexed to the Si-O-Si bond vibration [50], bands at 3600–3800, and 1695 cm−1 can be ascribed to the O–H stretching and deformation bands due to the Al-OH and Si-OH bonds, and water molecules. The bands reported between 2318 and 3225 cm−1 might be due to organic impurities present in granite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD pattern of granite waste powder
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern (Fig. 3 ) of granite powder reveals that granite consists of mainly; quartz (SiO2), anorthite (combination of Na2O, CaO, Al2O3, and SiO2), microcline (K2O.Al2O3.3SiO2) besides to albeit (due to CaO), anorthite (oxides of Na, Ca, Al, and Si), biotite where Fe2O3 is the major ingredient, and maghemite. The immense firmness of granite stone can be endorsed to the presence of an excess of quartz, moreover, the higher quantities of silica and alumina concurrently exist with the plentiful of aluminosilicate phases in the displayed XRD pattern [51].

The possible synthesis mechanism for PANI nanofibers is proposed and given in Scheme 1 . The FTIR spectrum analysis of granite powder, EDS of PANI nanofibers, and qualitative analysis of granite extract prove the presence of ferric ions (Fe3+) in granite extract. These Fe3+ ions initiated the oxidative polymerization of aniline monomer in an acidic medium. In the acidic medium, aniline converts into anilinium cation due to protonation which interacts with the Fe3+ ion and receives an electron from the Fe3+ ion due to this anilinium cation radical generates. Thus, the obtained anilinium cation radical converts into an aniline dimer that further interacts with Fe3+ ions and leads to conversion into dianilinium cation radical, which is act as a reaction propagator. The interaction of anilinium cation radical and dianilinium cation radical leads to create a polymer chain of PANI nanofibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers

ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 shows the typical XRD pattern of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers. The peaks obtained in the XRD pattern at 2θ = 14.72°, 20.84°, and 25.90° are the characteristics of the PANI polymer chain and can be indexed to the planes (001), (020), and (200), respectively [52]. The peaks observed at 20.84° and 25.90° are contributed to the infrequent repetition of the benzenoid and quinoid rings in the polymer chain of PANI, respectively [53]. The average particle size of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers is calculated using Debye Scherrer’s equation.
D =
0.9 λ
β c o s θ
where D denotes particle size, λ is the wavelength of radiation used (λ = 1.5406 Å), β represents to the full width at half maximum of the diffraction peaks and θ is Bragg's angle. The peak with maximum intensity is obtained at 2θ = 25.90°. The mean particle dimension computed using Debye Scherrer’s equation is 11.03 nm for PANI nanofibers.

Fig. 5 explains the FTIR spectrum of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers. The FTIR band detected at 3600–3800 cm−1 represents the O–H stretching vibrations and the band at 3200–3400 cm−1 is due to aromatic N–H stretching vibrations. Moreover, the absorption band observed at 2805–3058 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C–H aromatic bond [54]. The band accounted at 1744 cm−1 represents NH2 bending vibration, and peaks at 1503 and 1641 cm−1 are attributed to the C = C stretching vibrations of benzenoid and quinoid ring structures in the prepared nanofibers [54,55].

The endurance of C = N and C-N bonds in PANI nanofibers is confirmed from the FTIR bands observed at 1383 cm−1, and 1294 and 1245 cm−1 which are corresponding to the C = N stretching of –NH– and C-N stretching vibration of the benzenoid ring. Additionally, the bands observed at 1128 and 808 cm−1 are indexed to the aromatic C–H in-plane bending and out-of-plane bending vibrations of the 1, 4-disubstituted aromatic ring [54,55]. Fig. 6 depicts the UV–Vis. absorption spectrum for the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers which exhibited the two distinctive absorption bands related to the PANI polymer at 354 and 644 nm [54,56,57]. The first absorption band reported at 354 nm is corresponding to the π - π* electronic transitions in the benzenoid (B) ring while the second absorption band observed at 644 nm is endorsed to the (polaron-π*) polaron transitions [56]. Moreover, the band at 644 nm represents the protonation stages of the PANI polymer chain [54,57].

Fig. 7 (a-d) shows FESEM micrographs of the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers at different magnifications. The FESEM micrographs reveal that the GW-mediated synthesized PANI polymer is made up of porous and fibrous nanostructures, and the fiber particles are well-connected and found in the highly agglomerated fibrous network phase. It is conceivable since the generated PANI nanoparticles have high binding energy. Fig. 7 (e) illustrates the EDS spectrum for the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers, which include the peaks corresponding to the C, N, Fe, and Cl elements. The elemental composition of the constituent elements of the PANI nanofibers is displayed in Table as shown in the inset of Fig. 7(e). The source of Fe (iron) is granite waste which proves that Fe3+ was the main oxidizing species in the GWE. The Cl source is HCl utilized as a dopant acid. Fig. 8 (a-d) shows TEM images of the prepared PANI nanofibers at low and high magnification. The PANI nanofibers are strongly linked to one another and possess rough surfaces. Fig. 8(e-f) shows HRTEM micrographs of PANI nanofibers. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of the PANI nanofibers suggests that it is an amorphous or weakly crystalline in nature, which is evidenced by the presence of undefined dazzling electron diffraction rings (Fig. 8(e)).

The thermal stability of the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber was assessed by considering TGA and DTA curves. It can be seen that PANI nanofiber undertook a three-step disintegration pattern [58] as displayed in (Fig. 9 (a)). Initially, at a lower temperature range (25–155 °C) in the initial stage of degradation, 5.86% of weight loss in PANI nanofibers observed, which can be endorsed to the expulsion of the adsorbed water molecules from the polymer matrix [58,59]. In the second step, 25.60% of weight loss determined up to 402 °C, which is ascribed to the elimination of dopant and low molecular weight remnants [59,60]. In the third step of disintegration at 402 °C onwards 54.17% of weight loss due to the decomposition of the polyaniline backbone [59,60]. The DTA curve for PANI nanofibers exhibited an endothermic peak around 62 °C (inset of Fig. 9), which might be derived due to the discharge of the adsorbed H2O molecules.

The specific surface area, pore dimension, and pore size distribution (PSD) play a decisive task in the catalytic reduction process. The BET N2 gas adsorption–desorption analysis performed at 77 K was used to assess the porosity, pore size, and PSD of the produced PANI nanofibers, and the results are shown in Fig. 10 . The produced PANI nanofibers have a BET surface area of 30.239 m2g−1 and a pore diameter of 0.7053 nm. The N2 gas sorption isotherm for PANI nanofibers is observed of type IV isotherm [61], with H3 type hysteresis (P/P0 greater than 0.5), which is typical of mesoporous materials with slit-shape pores (Fig. 10(a)) [62]. Fig. 10(b) and Fig. 10(c) show the multipoint BET isotherm and Barett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution plots for the prepared fibers, respectively. The values of BET surface area, pore dimension, and volume are listed in Table 1 . The BJH pore size distribution projected that PANI nanofibers possess a pore distribution of nano-dimension range and reflected that PANI nanofibers hold fine porosity. The majority of pores are comprised of mesopores (2 < d < 50 nm) [63] and the average pore radius is estimated to be 6.61 nm. Additionally, a plot representing the volume of gas adsorbed as a function of statistical thickness is constructed and shown in Fig. 10(d). The large specific surface area and small average pore diameter result in more binding sites that enhance the easier binding of the pollutants at the surface of PANI nanofibers and favor catalytic reduction ability. Notably, more than 99% of the entire volume of pores is supplied from the mesopores as calculated employing the t-plot (Fig. 10(d)) method. The micropore surface area (Smicro), and external surface area (Sext) are estimated to be zero and 30.239 m2/g, respectively.

Zeta potential value furnishes information concerning the state of surface charge on the material surface and forecasts the long-term stability of colloidal systems [64]. Thus, to confirm the surface charge and colloidal stability of GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers, the zeta potential value was measured (Fig. 11 ). The zeta potential was estimated to be −6.49 mV, which is within the range of
± 30
m V
that recommended good long-term stability and low aggregation of nanofibers in the colloid system which reflects the better adsorption activity [65]. The negative charge on nanofibers surface is due to the presence of nitrogen-containing functionalities [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers

ce:section.ce:para: The reduction process of 4-NP was completely supervised by the UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The light yellow colored 4-NP displayed an absorbance peak at λ = 321 nm in UV − Vis. spectrum (Fig. 12 (a)). The catalytic reduction of 4-NP was noticed by measuring the alteration in the absorbance of 4-NP at 400 nm with the passing of reaction time, and it is authenticated that due to the reduction of 4-NP, a fresh peak at 301 nm was identified related to the 4-aminophenol (4-AP) [67]. On pouring NaBH4 solution into the 4-NP solution, the peak that appeared at 321 nm was shifted to 400 nm along with turning of the solution color from fade yellow to murky yellow due to the change of 4-NP into 4 nitrophenolate (4-NPate) ion [68]. In absence of the catalyst under normal conditions, the peak intensity at 400 nm was noticed to be stable for a long time and the fade yellow color of the 4-NP solution was also not faded up, which exhibited that the reduction reaction is extremely sluggish [69]. In 40 min of reaction time, only 2% of 4-NP was found to be reduced into 4-AP, thus, the catalytic reduction process of 4-NP in the absence of PANI nanofiber is very slow. But, on the addition of GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers to the reaction medium vigorous bubbles were produced from the solution with the fading of the yellow color of the reaction mixture, and a fresh peak at λ = 301 nm was found to be appeared corresponding to the 4-AP. In absence of a catalyst, the repulsion between the negatively charged 4-NPate and BH4 - ions hinders the electron transfer process and retard the reduction process [70]. The catalyst transports near to the contributor BH4 - and taker 4-NPate ions on the surface of the catalyst and assists in the electron transfer process from the giver (BH4 -) to the acceptor (4-NPate ion) that facilitated the reduction process.

Ultimately, after 40 min, over 15 mg of PANI nanofibers, the 4-NP solution was almost entirely transformed into a colorless solution and the peak at 400 nm was totally vanished (Fig. 12(a)), which revealed that the 4-NP is exclusively reduced to 4-AP while over 10 mg of PANI nanofibers, approx 86% was reduced. Moreover, the intensity of the absorption peak that emerged at 301 nm was found to be enhanced with the decrease of the intensity of the peak at 400 nm.

Fig. 12(b) displays the change of Ct/C0 with the reaction time for the catalytic reduction of 4-NP in presence of GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber catalyst exhibiting the reduction of 4-NP, where C0 and Ct represent the concentrations of 4-NP at the initial and time t, respectively.

The effect of polyaniline nanofibers on the reduction performance of 4-NP was investigated. In the absence of a polyaniline nanofiber catalyst, a very less amount of 4-NP was reduced into 4-NP in 40 min of reaction time, thus, no noticeable change in the color of 4-NP was observed. Moreover, without using NaBH4 solution in the presence of polyaniline nanofibers as shown in Table 2 (entry 2), no notable color alteration of 4-NP was detected which reveals that no reduction reaction occured. As displayed in Table 2 (entries 3, 4, 5) on using 5, 10, and 15 mg of PANI nanofiber catalyst with 10 mL (2 mM) of 4-NP and 10 mL (0.2 M) of NaBH4 solution, the utmost decline in absorbance of the 4-NP solution was observed for 15 mg of catalyst in 40 min of reaction time while with 10 mg of catalyst, approx 86% reduction reported. Thus, the best performance of the reduction process of 4-NP is determined over 15 mg PANI nanofiber catalyst in 40 min.

To comprehend the activity of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofiber catalyst, a kinetic study of the catalytic reduction reaction was carried out. The amount of NaBH4 in the reaction solution was taken high enough in comparison to the 4-NP concentration. It could be believed that the concentration of the NaBH4 in the reaction mixture stays invariable during the reaction and, thus, the rates of reduction reaction could be deemed reliant on the concentration of 4-NP and independent of the concentration of NaBH4. Hence, the degree of the reduction reaction is supposed to be investigated by just measuring the absorbance of the 4-NP at 400. As the rate of the reaction is reliant on the concentration of the 4-NP, the kinetics of the catalytic reduction reaction could be explained by employing the pseudo-first-order (PFO) kinetic model and kinetic rate constant (k) value for the catalytic reduction reaction of the 4-NP was from the slope of the plot drawn between the ln(Ct/Co) and reaction time (t). Fig. 13 illustrates the PFO kinetic plot for the catalytic reduction of 4-NP over the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber catalyst. The value of the kinetic rate constant for the catalytic reduction of the 4-NP was estimated to be 0.0488 min−1 or 8.13x10-4 s−1 with the 10 mg of PANI nanofibers. The closeness of the regression coefficient (R2) value to the unity for the catalytic reduction of 4-NP (0.954) declares the correctness of the PFO kinetic model for the catalytic reduction of the 4-NP with NaBH4 in presence of granite waste-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers.

The catalytic reduction efficiency of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofiber catalyst concerning the 4-nitrophenol was matched with other catalysts as displayed in Table 3 , which reflected that the prepared nanofiber showed fine activity toward the reduction of the 4-NP.

The recyclability test is essential to investigate the catalytic stability of the prepared catalyst and it is vital for practical applications of a catalyst. Herein, the catalytic stability of the prepared PANI nanofibers was monitored by performing a catalytic reduction reaction of 4-NP over PANI nanofibers in five successive cycles. As shown in Fig. 14 the GW-mediated fabricated PANI nanofibers exhibited roughly invariable catalytic activity with no apparent decline in percentage reduction of 4-NP even after five cycles, indicating the admirable constancy in activity and good stability of the synthesized nanofiber catalyst for the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic reduction of Rhodamine B dye
ce:section.ce:para: RhB is a cationic organic dye that is broadly utilized in lots of industries. It is unsafe and mutagenic in nature. The aqueous medium RhB demonstrates an absorption peak at 554 nm (Fig. 15 (a)) [77]. The change in the peak intensity at 554 nm was taken into consideration to examine the entire reduction process of RhB dye in presence of NaBH4 solution over GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers. As demonstrated in Fig. 15(a), in presence of the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber catalyst and NaBH4 a gradual decrease in the peak intensity at 554 nm for RhB dye solution was reported with the disappearance of pink color. After 5 min the pink color of the reaction solution completely disappeared with 15 mg of PANI nanofibers and it turned colorless. However, in absence of a catalyst, the peak intensity at 554 nm was found to be unaltered and the pink color stayed unchanged for a long time. As ever the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber catalyst was mixed with dye solution an intense bubbling started with the decolorization of the solution, and the peak intensity was found to be decreased. After 5 min of reaction time, the absorption peak at 554 nm completely disappeared and the solution turned colorless due to the reduction of pink-colored RhB into leuco-rhodamine B (LRhB) which is colorless. Fig. 15(b) exhibits the deviation of Ct/C0 with reaction time for the catalytic reduction of RhB in presence of GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber catalyst exhibiting the reduction of RhB.

In the absence of a polyaniline nanofiber catalyst, only a 3% of reduction of RhB took place in 60 min of reaction time and no noticeable change in color was observed. Moreover, without using NaBH4 solution in the presence of polyaniline nanofibers as shown in Table 4 (entry 2), no reduction reaction occurred in 60 min of reaction time. As displayed in Table 4 (entries 3, 4, 5) on using 5, 10, and 15 mg of catalyst, the utmost decline in absorbance of RhB solution was observed for 15 mg of catalyst in 5 min while with 10 mg of PANI nanofibers, 61% reduction took place. Thus, the best performance of the reduction process of 4-NP is determined over 15 mg PANI nanofiber catalyst in 5 min.

To probe the kinetics of the catalytic reduction reaction of RhB dye over PANI nanofiber catalyst in presence of NaBH4 the PFO kinetic plot was drawn between ln(Ct/Co) vs. time (t) and the value of kinetic rate constant was estimated from the slope of the curve. Fig. 16 shows the kinetic plot for the catalytic reduction reaction of RhB dye over PANI nanofiber catalyst in presence of NaBH4. The value of the kinetic rate constant was estimated to be 0.958 min−1 or 15.97 x10-3 s−1 with 15 mg of PANI nanofibers and the regression coefficient (R2) value was determined to be 0.954 which reveals good proximity with the unity that proved the suitability of the PFO kinetic model for the catalytic reduction of the RhB with NaBH4 in presence of granite waste mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers. The catalytic reduction capability of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofiber catalyst for the reduction of RhB dye was matched by other synthesized catalysts and shown in Table 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for the catalytic reduction of 4-NP and RhB dye
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible reaction mechanism for the catalytic reductions of the 4-NP and RhB dye by NaBH4 in the presence of the GW-mediated synthesized PANI nanofiber catalyst is proposed and presented in Fig. 17 . In an aqueous solution, NaBH4 hydrolyses to generate the BH4 - ions which adsorb on the surface of PANI nanofiber catalyst and transfer electrons to the catalyst and generate active hydrogen. Concurrently, the adsorption of substrate molecules (4-NP and RhB) also occur on the surface of the catalyst. Thus, the electron transfer process occurs on the surface of the catalyst from BH4 - (donor) to substrate molecules (acceptor 4-NP and RhB), where the catalyst acts as the electron relay center. Since the water contaminants, 4-NP and RhB used in the current study possess aromatic rings in their structures and the GW –mediated prepared PANI polymer chains also constituted with the quinoid and benzenoid rings and nitrogen-containing functionalities (as discussed in the FTIR section) in their structures, thus, the pollutants get adsorbed on the catalyst surface via pi–pi interactions [78], H-bonding, and electrostatic interactions.

Finally, the interaction of intermediate species with the active hydrogen atoms generated by BH4 - completes the reduction process of the 4-NP or RhB into 4-aminophenol (4-AP) or leuco-rhodamine (LRhB). The reduced products (4-AP) or LRhB disengage from the PANI nanofiber catalyst surface and complete the reduction cycle while the regenerated PANI nanofiber catalyst enters into a fresh catalytic cycle.

In summary, PANI nanofibers were prepared via a facile, environmentally benign, and sustainable approach using granite waste produced at granite mining, processing, and application sites as an oxidizing agent. The prepared PANI nanofibers were characterized by using various analytical techniques such as XRD, FESEM-EDS, HRTEM, FTIR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, BET, TGA-DTA, and Zeta potential. The prepared nanofiber possessed a mesoporous structure that exhibited type IV N2 gas sorption isotherm with type H3 hysteresis which is characteristic of mesoporous material with slit-shape pores, and high specific surface area (30.239 m2 g−1) and pore volume (0.2317 nm).

The granite waste-mediated synthesized PANI nanofibers were utilized for the catalytic reductive degradation of hazardous organic water toxins 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and Rhodamine B (RhB) dye in aqueous phases in presence of NaBH4 solution. The prepared PANI nanofiber catalyst demonstrated excellent catalytic reductive performance for the 4-NP and RhB due to the high porosity, specific surface area, pore volume, and small pore diameter. With the 10 mg of PANI nanofibers, 85.79 % of 4-NP was reduced whereas with 15 mg of catalyst approx 100% reduced in 40 min of reaction time. Similarly, in presence of 10 and 15 mg of PANI catalyst 61 and approx 99% of RhB dye reduced in five min of reaction time. Furthermore, the kinetics of the reduction reactions followed pseudo-first-order, and the kinetic rate constant values were estimated to be 0.0488 and 0.958 min−1 for 4-NP and RhB, respectively. Thus, the current plan of study help in discarding the wastes generated from granite mining, processing, and application sites that might be beneficial for the lessening of pollution load due to GW, and the application of the GW-mediated prepared PANI nanofibers as an efficient and valuable material for the environmental remediation purposes, especially water purification. Furthermore, the current study adds a new chapter toward environmental remediation and sustainability. Thus, employing this novel, environmentally benign, and sustainable approach to produce valuable material using wastes generated from natural resources and its utilization for the remediation of water pollution might help to resolve the environmental issues in two ways one is the direct consumption of granite wastes which is generating serious environmental problems and another is an indirect use of granite waste for the generation of precious material which can be utilized for the exclusion of a variety of water pollutants from the polluted water.
